title: Dog Hot Spot Oozing Pus on Hip: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Find out what to do if your dog has an oozing hot spot on their hip. Learn about urgency levels and how a photo can help your vet provide treatment.
content_html:
What is an Oozing Hot Spot?

A hot spot, medically known as acute moist dermatitis, is a condition where a localized area of skin becomes severely inflamed and infected. When you see pus, it indicates that a secondary bacterial infection has developed. These spots are often triggered by allergies, insect bites, or trapped moisture, and are made worse by the dog's persistent licking and chewing of the hip area.

Urgency Level: Medium

An oozing hot spot is classified as a medium-urgency situation. While it is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, the presence of pus means the infection is active and likely painful. Without veterinary treatment, such as antibiotics or professional-grade topical medication, the infection can spread rapidly across the skin or lead to deeper tissue issues. You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear, well-lit photograph of the lesion on the hip is a vital step in the triage process. Hot spots can change dramatically in a matter of hours; a photo provides a baseline for your vet to monitor the rate of spread. Additionally, sharing a photo with your clinic's triage team can help them determine if your dog needs to be squeezed in for an earlier appointment or if specific at-home precautions should be taken immediately.

faqs.question: Can I use hydrogen peroxide on the oozing spot?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid hydrogen peroxide as it can damage healthy skin cells and delay the healing process. It is safer to use a mild, vet-approved antiseptic or simply keep the area dry until your appointment.

faqs.question: How do I stop the hot spot from spreading?
faqs.answer: The most important step is to prevent your dog from licking or biting the hip. Using an Elizabethan collar (cone) is the most effective way to break the itch-lick cycle and prevent the infection from worsening.

faqs.question: Will my dog need antibiotics?
faqs.answer: If the hot spot is oozing pus, it is highly likely that your veterinarian will prescribe either oral antibiotics or a potent medicated topical spray to resolve the bacterial infection.
